coredata.dc:description: Humic acid (HA), a widely prevalent natural organic matter in aquatic environments, poses serious health risks due to its potential to form carcinogenic by-products during water treatment. While the photo-Fenton process can be employed for HA degradation, its effectiveness is often hindered by the poor catalytic performance of conventional catalysts. In this study, Co3O4 was modified with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) and immobilized on a PVDF membrane. Compared with Co3O4/PVDF, Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF system achieved 85.1 % HA removal and 66 % mineralization within 60 min through photocatalytic peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation as well as showed excellent stability in cycle experiments. The incorporation of NCDs enhanced the synergistic effect between light and PMS, improved light absorption due to the narrowed bandgap of Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF, and played a significant role in generating the primary reactive species, 1O2. Additionally, Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF effectively controlled Co ions leaching. Finally, the degradation intermediates were identified, and the degradation pathways were proposed. The photo-Fenton degradation of HA preferentially broke unsaturated bonds and degraded aromatic ring compounds. The Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF photo-Fenton catalytic system serves as an effective approach for boosting the efficiency of photo-Fenton degradation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Humic acid (HA), a widely prevalent natural organic matter in aquatic environments, poses serious health risks due to its potential to form carcinogenic by-products during water treatment. While the photo-Fenton process can be employed for HA degradation, its effectiveness is often hindered by the poor catalytic performance of conventional catalysts. In this study, Co3O4 was modified with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) and immobilized on a PVDF membrane. Compared with Co3O4/PVDF, Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF system achieved 85.1 % HA removal and 66 % mineralization within 60 min through photocatalytic peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation as well as showed excellent stability in cycle experiments. The incorporation of NCDs enhanced the synergistic effect between light and PMS, improved light absorption due to the narrowed bandgap of Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF, and played a significant role in generating the primary reactive species, 1O2. Additionally, Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF effectively controlled Co ions leaching. Finally, the degradation intermediates were identified, and the degradation pathways were proposed. The photo-Fenton degradation of HA preferentially broke unsaturated bonds and degraded aromatic ring compounds. The Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF photo-Fenton catalytic system serves as an effective approach for boosting the efficiency of photo-Fenton degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Humic acid (HA) is a prevalent type of natural organic matter (NOM) found widely in aquatic environments (Wang et al., 2024; Sillanpää et al., 2018). It originates from the decomposition of plant and animal matter and is characterized by its complex structure and high molecular weight. Notably, HA can interact with disinfectants, such as chlorine, during water treatment processes, leading to the formation of carcinogenic by-products like trihalomethanes (THMs) and haloacetic acids (HAAs) (Park et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). These by-products pose significant health risks, including an increased likelihood of cancer and adverse reproductive outcomes. Therefore, effectivity removing the HA is crucial in water treatment (Ryu et al., 2021). Several approaches, including biological, physical, and chemical methods, have been developed and optimized for HA removal. These include biodegradation (Luo et al., 2022; Zahmatkesh et al., 2016), adsorption (Wang et al., 2021), coagulation (Yue et al., 2021), filtration (Särkkä et al., 2015), electrochemical oxidation (Yan et al., 2022), Fenton oxidation (Huang et al., 2017), and photocatalysis (Oskoei et al., 2016).

Recently, photo-Fenton technology has gained widespread attention due to its cost-effectiveness and the sustainable utilization of light as an energy source (Liu et al., 2024; Ma et al., 2024). This technology generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and sulfate radicals (SO₄·-), through the activation of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) in photocatalysis. These ROS possess strong oxidative capabilities, enabling the efficient degradation of HA (Banik and Basumallick, 2017). However, the poor catalytic performance of photocatalysts is a significant challenge, limiting the overall efficiency of the photo-PMS process.

Currently, transition metal catalysts are extensively studied for their effective activation of PMS to generate ROS, attributed to their high performance, ease of operation, and low energy requirements (Cai et al., 2020). Ahn et al. (2019) evaluated various transition metals as PMS activators and determined that cobalt exhibited the highest PMS activation efficiency (Ji et al., 2020). Co3O4 is a widely used and effective catalyst due to its outstanding catalytic activity, chemical stability, and unique electronic and magnetic properties (Guo et al., 2022). However, conventional Co3O4 catalysts suffer from a limited specific surface area, necessitating further modification (Subagyo et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2022).

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs), a novel cocatalyst with a large specific surface area (Hu et al., 2023; Tian et al., 2015), can enhance visible light absorption (Zhou et al., 2020)and improve the photocatalytic performance of Co3O4 through surface modification (Li et al., 2018). While NCDs have been effectively utilized in electrochemistry, particularly as high-performance electrode materials, their application in catalyzing the degradation of organic pollutants in water remains underexplored (Ji et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2020; Arjunan et al., 2023; Adekoya et al., 2020). In our previous work, NCDs modified Co3O4 was employed as a catalyst for developing photo-Fenton membrane distillation membranes, yet the degradation mechanism of this catalyst has not to be elucidated (Lu et al., 2024).

In this work, NCDs was introduced to Co3O4 modification for HA removal, and the degradation mechanism in the photo-Fenton catalytic system was further elucidated. The effects of the introduction of NCDs on the chemical and optical properties as well as the catalytic properties of materials were compared, and the possible HA degradation mechanisms were explored. This work would broaden the application of NCDs as cocatalysts and provide viable strategies for designing efficient photo-Fenton processes.

Ethanol absolute (EtOH), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), furfuryl alcohol (FFA), and trihalomethanes (THMs) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. HA and PMS (KHSO5•0.5KHSO4•0.5 K2SO4) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

Both NCDs and Co3O4 were fabricated by hydrothermal reaction. The catalyst was incorporated into a PVDF membrane (IPVH00010, Millipore Company, USA) through a filtration loading method so that it can be easily recovered and reused (Wang et al., 2019). The detailed preparation processes for the resulting catalysts (Co3O4/PVDF and Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF) can be found in Text S1.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI, Tecnai G2 F30, USA) was employed to observe the morphology of the materials. The chemical properties of the materials were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Nicolet IS50, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo-VG Scientific, ESCALAB250, USA). The optical properties of the materials were characterized using an ultraviolet-visible-near-infrared (UV-Vis-NIR) spectrophotometer (Agilent, Cary 7000, USA). UV254 absorbance was measured using an ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectrophotometer (Puxi, T6, China). The mineralization rate of HA was determined using a total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer (SHIMADZU, TOC-L, Japan). The cobalt leaching concentration was determined using inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, PerkinElmer, OPTIMA 8000, USA).

An Excitation-Emission-Matrix (EEM) spectrometer (Edinburgh, FS5, UK) was employed to obtain EEM maps, which were divided into five regions for fluorescence regional integration (EEM-FRI) (Chen et al., 2003). Each region represents a class of organic matter: (I) aromatic proteinaceous substances I (tryptophan), (II) aromatic proteinaceous substances II (tyrosine), (III) fulvic acid-like substances, (IV) dissolved microbial by-products, and (V) humic acid-like substances (Yu et al., 2023). Text S5 provides detailed calculations of the intensity and percentage of fluorescent components within the organic matter corresponding to each region. The products generated from the photo-Fenton degradation of HA were further analyzed using a Gas Chromatograph-Mass Spectrometer (GC-MS, Hexin, GGT 0620, China). Quenching experiments were performed by adding EtOH, TBA, FFA, and THMs, which served as scavengers for SO4 ·-, ·OH, singlet oxygen (1O2), and superoxide radical (O2 ·-), respectively (Detailed procedures are provided in Text S6). Furthermore, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was used to identify ROS.

As shown in Fig. 1, the HR-TEM images and XRD patterns of Co3O4 and Co3O4/NCDs revealed that the main lattice fringe spacing on the surface of Co3O4 was 0.240 nm, which corresponded to the (311) crystal plane of Co3O4(Cao et al., 2021). Furthermore, a fringe spacing of 0.201 nm was observed, which was attributed to the incorporation of NCDs (Yan et al., 2017).

Due to the difficulty in separating and recovering powdered catalysts from the reaction system, Co3O4 and Co3O4/NCDs were loaded onto PVDF membrane for further characterization to investigate their chemical and optical properties.

FTIR spectroscopy was employed to identify the functional groups present in the prepared materials. As shown in Fig. 2a, the characteristic peaks of Co3O4/PVDF at 655 cm−1 and 553 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of Co2+O4 in the tetrahedral position and the vibration of Co3+O6 in the octahedral position, respectively (Rabee et al., 2022). Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF showed slight vibrations at 3500 cm−1 and 3376 cm−1, which were attributed to O-H stretching (Massad-Ivanir et al., 2018). The characteristic peak appearing at 1341 cm−1 was likely associated with the existence of C-O-C chemical bonds in NCDs (Liu et al., 2020a), and the existence of CO bonds caused vibration at 1548 cm−1 (Hui et al., 2021).

XPS was employed to further characterize the surface chemical composition of prepared materials. As shown in Fig. 2b, Co3O4/PVDF was primarily composed of C, F, Co, and O elements. Among them, F element corresponded to C-F bonds of PVDF, and no peaks of other elements were detected, indicating that the prepared Co3O4 had high purity. In Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF spectrum, an additional characteristic peak of N 1 s was observed, originating from tertiary nitrogen (N-(C)3) groups, indicating the incorporation of NCDs (Hong et al., 2018). In C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c), the peak at 289.76 eV appearing was caused by the O-CO bond (Deng et al., 2017). When the NCDs were doped, the binding energy positions at which the peaks appeared were all shifted to varying degrees and similar results were also observed in O 1 s and Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 2d-e). Some mutual binding between Co3O4 and NCDs had occurred (Liu et al., 2020b), indicating modifications in the electronic structure of Co3O4(Santoni et al., 2017). In O 1 s spectrum, the peak at 530.04 eV was caused by the O-Co chemical bond (Lin et al., 2021), which may originate from the Co3O4. The Co 2p spectrum displayed two large characteristic peaks at about 795 eV and 780 eV, representing the spin-orbit peaks of Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2, respectively (Fan et al., 2022). Two sub-peaks were observed at 795.99 eV and 794.48 eV in the spin-orbit peak of Co 2p1/2, corresponding to Co2+ and Co3+. A similar situation also occurred in the spin-orbit peak of Co 2p3/2, and the peaks at 781.31 eV and 779.70 eV were generated by Co2+ and Co3+, respectively (Zhao et al., 2020, 2019).

The light absorption capacity of the prepared materials in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm was analyzed using UV-Vis-NIR spectroscopy ( Fig. 3a-b). After doping NCDs, the absorption peak intensity of Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF further improved compared to Co3O4/PVDF. In particular, in the visible light range, the absorption peak intensity increased to 0.9–1.0, indicating that the incorporation of NCDs significantly enhanced the light absorption capacity of Co3O4. Furthermore, the bandgap energy of the prepared materials was calculated using the Tauc equation (Nayak et al., 2022). After doping NCDs in Co3O4, the forbidden bandwidth decreased from the original 1.99 eV to 1.55 eV, suggesting that photoexcited electrons can more efficiently transition from NCDs to Co3O4(Zaman et al., 2024). This resulted in increased light absorption capacity and photogenerated electron transfer efficiency of catalytic materials, and further improved the catalytic performance in photo-PMS activation and pollutant degradation.

As shown in Fig. 4a-b, the degradation of HA in different experimental groups primarily occurred within the first 30 min, after which the degradation rate plateaued. In contrast, Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF exhibited the highest HA degradation rate of 85.1 % within 60 min, indicating that the incorporation of NCDs significantly enhanced the HA degradation efficiency. The catalytic performance of Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF was compared with various catalysts reported in the literature, as summarized in Table 1. The results demonstrated that our synthesized Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF exhibited excellent catalytic performance. It was also interesting to observe that the k
obs for light + Co3O4/PVDF was negative. This phenomenon arose because the concentration of HA appeared to increase during the reaction, as indicated by the rising intensity of peaks A and B in the EEM spectra (See Section 3.4), attributing to the excitation effect of PMS on fluorescent substances that were initially masked within the HA structure (Liu et al., 2022). This excitation led to an enhanced fluorescence signal and, consequently, an increase in UV254 absorbance, which may be misinterpreted as an apparent increase in HA concentration.

The degradation synergy coefficients between light and PMS were calculated based on the observed k
obs of each group, and the results are as follows:

If the calculated degradation synergy coefficient is greater than 1, it indicates a synergistic effect between the photoactivation and PMS activation of the materials. The larger the value, the stronger the synergy. Conversely, a coefficient less than 1 indicates antagonistic interactions between the two processes (Karim and Shriwastav, 2020). The calculated results indicated a significant synergistic relationship between light and PMS in the degradation of HA by Co3O4/PVDF and Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF. Additionally, the reaction coefficient increased from 6.17 to 7.90 upon the incorporation of NCDs.

As shown in Fig. 4c, the fastest PMS depletion among the three experimental groups occurred within the first 15 min, and the depletion rate did not significantly increase after 30 min, consistent with the HA degradation results. The consumption rate of PMS in the light + PMS + Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF group was nearly 100 % at 15 min, whereas that of the other two groups was only 75 % and 29 %, which indicated that the incorporation of NCDs was able to fully activate PMS to degrade HA. The changes in TOC values within the solution during the degradation of HA in each experimental group were measured to calculate the changes in HA mineralization rate, as shown in Fig. 4d. In the light + PMS + PVDF blank group, the HA mineralization rate was only 16.6 % during the 60-minute degradation process due to the low efficiency of PMS activation. The HA mineralization rate was significantly enhanced in the two experimental groups containing catalysts, increasing to 53.2 % and 66 %, respectively. The mineralization rate increased faster during the early stages of degradation (0–30 min), which was attributed to the fact that the consumption of PMS was mainly concentrated in that time period. However, PMS was almost entirely consumed by the catalyst at a later stage, making it difficult to further increase the mineralization rate.

The stability of the prepared materials was further explored. As shown in Fig. 4e, cycling experiments were conducted under different reaction conditions. Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF exhibited high HA removal efficiency and maintained good stability at pH = 7 and PMS dosages of 5 mM and 10 mM. When the pH was lowered to 3.0, the degradation efficiency decreased, but the catalyst still demonstrated stable performance. This decrease in efficiency was likely due to the presence of H⁺, which can interfere with the activation of PMS by Co3O4. Specifically, HSO₅⁻ may interact with Co3O4, leading to the formation of by-products, while hydrogen bonding between H⁺ and the O-O bond of HSO₅⁻ can further inhibit PMS activation (Zheng et al., 2019).

Furthermore, the leaching of Co ions from the prepared materials was monitored during the photo-Fenton reaction. As shown in Fig. 4f, the incorporation of NCDs effectively inhibited the leaching of Co ions from Co3O4. This reduction in Co2+ concentration in the reaction system ruled out the role of Co2+ as a major contributor to the PMS activation (Zhang et al., 2023).

The XPS spectra of prepared materials before and after the photo-Fenton reaction are shown in Fig. 5a-b. After 30 min of the photo-Fenton reaction, the characteristic peak positions of Co2+ and Co3+ in Co3O4/PVDF and Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF shifted, and their peak area intensities changed. The peak areas of the Co2+ and Co3+ characteristic peaks were summed and analyzed as percentages ( Table 2). The Co valence transition during photo-Fenton reaction promoted the generation of ROS (e.g., ·OH and 1O2) (Peng et al., 2021), as illustrated in Eqs. (1) - (3).

According to these equations, Co(III) and Co(II) undergo a cyclic redox process, and their ratio generally remains stable. However, since the primary ROS in the photo-Fenton process of Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF was 1O2 (See Section 3.3.2), a significant amount of 1O2 was consumed. This caused Eq. (3) to shift towards the right, resulting in a higher proportion of Co(II) during the reaction. Consequently, the proportion of Co(II) increased from 43.96 % to 49.61 % after 60 min of HA degradation using Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF. During the reaction, Co(III) accepted electrons from PMS to produce SO5 ·-, which subsequently contributed to the generation of 1O2 through reaction with water. However, Co3O4/PVDF exhibited the opposite trend. After the reaction, the proportion of Co(Ⅱ) decreased from 44.39 % to 39.20 % which indicated that this reaction rate was low and can not generate a sufficient amount of 1O2 during the reaction of Co3O4/PVDF with PMS.

The primary active species in the light + PMS + Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF system were identified through quenching experiments. As shown in Fig. 6a, the degradation rate of HA decreased to varying degrees with the addition of all four quenchers. The degree of inhibition of HA degradation rate was similar when EtOH and TBA were added, indicating that SO4 ·- was not the primary active species in the photo-Fenton reaction. Since 1O2 was quenched by FFA, the degradation rate of HA decreased most significantly with the addition of FFA, indicating that 1O2 played an important role in the photo-Fenton reaction. Additional quenching experiments were performed in the absence of NCDs to further validate the role of 1O2 (Fig. S4). When FFA was used to quench 1O2, the HA removal rate was 40 % for Co3O4/PVDF and only 5.5 % for Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF. This indicated that a higher proportion of ¹O₂ was quenched in the Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF system, which significantly hindered HA degradation, confirming that ¹O₂ played a dominant role in this reaction. In contrast, in the Co3O4/PVDF system (without NCDs), FFA quenching had a much smaller effect, and HA degradation proceeded efficiently, suggesting that other reactive species—such as SO4 ·- radicals—played a more significant role in this case. These findings confirmed that the introduction of NCDs enhanced ¹O₂ production and shifted the dominant degradation pathway.

The contribution of each reactive species was calculated based on the observed k
obs of HA after the addition of different quenchers (Karimian et al., 2020), and the contributions of 1O2, ·OH, O2 ·-, and SO4 ·- were 41.84 %, 34.44 %, 23.21 %, and 0.51 %, respectively. Therefore, both radicals and non-radicals played a role during the photo-Fenton degradation of light + PMS + Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF group. 1O2 played a dominant role which was responsible for the realization of efficient HA degradation by Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF. This was in agreement with previous findings that 1O2 is the primary reactive species in PMS-involved photo-Fenton processes (Jia et al., 2024).

ESR spectroscopy was employed to further analyze the signal intensity of each ROS (Fig. 6b-d). At 0 min, the illumination had not yet been turned on, there was only the interaction between PMS and Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF, and no obvious signal peak intensity was detected. When the light was turned on and continued for up to 5 and 15 min, signal intensity appeared and increased with increasing light duration. As shown in Fig. 6c-d, a strong 1:1:1:1 quadruple state signal peak and a 1:1:1 triplet state signal peak were detected, corresponding to O2 ·- and 1O2, respectively (Zhao et al., 2025). The intensity of these peaks increased significantly with continued activation. A distinct 1:2:2:1 quadruple state signal peak corresponding to ·OH was also observed (Zhao et al., 2025), accompanied by a weak SO₄·- signal peak compared to other radical intensities. This finding was consistent with the previous quenching experiment, which showed that SO₄·- had the lowest contribution rate.

HA has been reported to have two distinct main peaks in the EEM spectra (Tian et al., 2018), and accordingly 200–550 nm was selected as the EEM test range. The EEM spectra of the HA solution (Fig. S5) exhibited two distinct characteristic peaks, A and B, representing humic acid-like organic matter. In light + PMS + PVDF blank group ( Fig. 7a), the intensities of peaks A and B first increased and then decreased. The intensities of peaks A and B increased from 15 to 30 min, which can be attributed to the excitation effect of PMS on masked fluorescent substances in HA (Liu et al., 2022). The intensities of peaks A and B gradually decreased from 30 to 60 min, corresponding to the HA removal curve, indicating that the photo-Fenton degradation process preferentially broke unsaturated bonds and degraded aromatic ring structures. In the light + PMS + Co3O4/PVDF group (Fig. 7b), the intensity of the characteristic peaks of HA-like substances reached their maximum 15 min earlier than in the blank group. Compared to the blank group, HA underwent the following three processes: (1) excitation of masked fluorophores; (2) disruption of unsaturated bonds in HA; and (3) degradation of HA. Furthermore, the characteristic peaks of HA almost disappeared at 60 min, indicating the effective degradation of HA-like substances. As shown in Fig. 7c, a more efficient degradation process was be observed in light + PMS + Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF group. Peaks A and B only showed a decreasing trend in intensity, which indicated that the reaction process of excitation of masked fluorescent substances was completed within 15 min. After 30 min of degradation, the intensity of peak A completely disappeared and only a small amount of peak B intensity remained. At this point, the position of peak B shifted left by approximately 20 nm, indicating its conversion into smaller molecular weight organic compounds. Combined with EEM-FRI (Fig. S6), the changes of each fluorescent component during HA degradation were explored in a more intuitive manner. For the light + PMS + PVDF blank group, the total fluorescence intensity of HA during degradation showed a tendency of increasing and then decreasing. The light + PMS + Co3O4/PVDF and light + PMS + Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF groups showed the same trend during the initial 0–60 min of degradation. The increase in the fluorescence intensity of component I (tryptophan analogs) indicated that most HA analogs were converted into small molecular weight organic matter towards the end of degradation, with component I being the main degradation product of HA. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity values of HA-like substances, primarily corresponding to components III and V, were significantly reduced at 60 min of degradation, indicating effective degradation of HA-like substances. The initial combined percentage of fluorescent components III and V was 82 %, which eventually decreased to 77 %, 27 %, and 20 % after 60 min of degradation in the light + PMS + PVDF, light + PMS + Co3O4/PVDF, and light + PMS + Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF groups, respectively, corresponding to the observed HA degradation trends.

The changes in each fluorescence component during the photo-Fenton degradation in the light + PMS + Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF system were further analyzed to elucidate the transformation pathways, as shown in Fig. 8a. The percentage of component V continued to decrease throughout the degradation process, while I component showed an opposite trend. Therefore, it can be inferred that components V and I were the initial and final products of the degradation process, respectively. The intensity percentages of the other three fluorescent components all initially increased and then decreased during the degradation process, suggesting that these components were intermediate products of the reaction. In the degradation process, large molecular weight substances were first degraded and transformed into smaller organic substances in a further reaction. Among these three components, the inflection point of component III appeared first, suggesting it was a smaller molecular weight HA substance and the first intermediate product to be formed. It was also observed that the increase in the total fluorescence intensity values occurring in the early stages of degradation was due to the increase in the content of component III. It can be speculated that the degradation of HA substances was to first destroy the unsaturated bonds within HA, convert them into fulvic acid substances with smaller molecular weight, and then undergo further degradation. The HA substances in component V were primarily macromolecular hydrophobic organic compounds. Previous research by Zhu et al. (2018). found that this type of materials was preferentially degraded and introduced many oxygen-containing functional groups, further triggering the cleavage of HA molecules. The inflection points of components II and IV were delayed until 30 min, suggesting that they were lower-level degradation products that may further degrade into fluorescent component I substances.

GC-MS was employed to further analyze the intermediates of HA degradation. Fig. 8b exhibited that the degradation products of HA were complex in composition, and 687 organic substances can be directly detected. Due to the large number of detected substances, the main intermediates of HA degradation were identified by ranking the peak area intensities of each compound (Fig. 8c). The molecular size of HA generally ranges from 2000 to 10,000 Da (Maia et al., 2008), and its carbon chain structure consists mainly of aliphatic chains, aromatic groups and furan groups (Jansen et al., 2006). It contains a variety of organic functional groups, with ester bonds, ether bonds, carboxyl groups, ketones, aldehydes, and alcohol groups predominantly present (McDonald et al., 2004). Judging by diversity in humus structure, degradation intermediates exhibit a wide range of diversity in size and structure. Observation in the order of molecular size showed that large molecules of organic matter underwent a series of reactions, such as breaking and ring opening, which ultimately leaded to the formation of small molecules of organic matter, such as alcohols and carboxylic acids, which were partially mineralized into CO2 and H2O. From Fig. 8c, it can be found that carboxylic acids mainly included C8H10O2 (MW = 138) and C5H7NO2 (MW = 113), and alcohols mainly included C26H40O5 (MW = 432) and C19H18O7 (MW = 358). These degradation products all contained oxygen-containing functional groups with strong polarity, which can easily combine with water molecules and exhibit hydrophilic properties. Jansen et al (Jansen et al., 2006). studied the oxidation of HA by ozone and hypothesized that the degradation of HA is an “outside-in” pruning process. They believed that due to the presence of a large number of double bonds within the carbon structure, the backbone structure at the center of the molecule was relatively stable, and the cleavage of small molecules occurred mainly at the periphery of the HA molecule, which was slowly eroded. Due to the stability of the internal structure of the HA molecule, it was difficult to be destroyed by further reactions, and therefore it was difficult to further increase the mineralization rate of HA. No organic substances with molecular weights lower than 100 Da were found in the mass spectrometry search results of HA degradation products.

This study successfully developed an efficient Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF photo-Fenton catalytic system which achieved 85.1 % HA removal and 66 % mineralization within 60 min through PMS activation, demonstrating a significant enhancement in photo-Fenton catalytic efficiency through the incorporation of NCDs as a cocatalyst. Compared with a single material, Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF had excellent stability and light-absorbing ability, significantly enhancing the synergistic activation of light and PMS in the photo-Fenton process. The results of quenching experiments and ESR showed that 1O2 played a major role in photo-Fenton degradation of HA. The development of Co3O4/NCDs/PVDF photo-Fenton catalytic system provides a reference strategy for enhancing the photo-Fenton degradation.
